channel.title: The Grant Desk - US
channel.description: Paid open calls, fellowships and residencies in AI, tech, research, and digital and mixed-media arts, sorted into the right pile. Updated as new calls land on the desk. Filtered to: US.

item.title: The Cloud House Residency
item.description:
Organisation: Dreamsong

Location: Minneapolis, Minnesota, USA

Award: USD 300 per week stipend (prorated), plus free accommodation in a 700 sq ft one-bedroom cottage; possible studio space and travel/materials budget on request USD 30 application fee (plus USD 500 refundable deposit upon acceptance)

Duration: 4 to 6 weeks (one-week stays available periodically)

Deadline: Rolling / undated

The Cloud House Residency, run by Dreamsong in the Northeast Arts District of Minneapolis, provides accommodation and support for individuals working in contemporary art who intend to develop, exhibit, or curate new work in Minnesota. Residents stay in a 700 square foot one-bedroom cottage on Dreamsong's compound and receive a prorated stipend of USD 300 per week, with possible studio space and travel or materials budgets considered on request. The program is open to emerging and mid-career visual artists, curators, and writers with a focus on contemporary art, and it hosts one individual or a two-person collaborative team at a time. The organizers arrange introductions to local artists, curators, writers, institutions, and galleries across the Twin Cities. Standard stays are 4 to 6 weeks, with occasional one-week options. Applications are reviewed on a rolling basis, and the program is currently booking residents for October through December 2026 and throughout 2027.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Kaatsbaan Subsidized Residencies
item.description:
Organisation: Kaatsbaan Cultural Park

Location: Tivoli, New York, USA

Award: Generously subsidized residency; includes hotel-style on-campus rooms, locally sourced meals, and access to studios, a 160-seat black box theater, and a 153-acre Hudson Valley campus. No artist stipend.

Duration: Individually designed, typically 5 to 21 days

Deadline: Rolling / undated

Kaatsbaan Cultural Park offers subsidized residencies for artists of all genres to create, live, and collaborate on its 153-acre campus in the Hudson Valley near Tivoli, New York. Residencies are individually designed between the artist, Kaatsbaan, and partners to fit the needs of the artist or company, welcoming disciplines from dance, theater, music, and visual arts to writing, composition, design, and film. The program is generously subsidized and includes individual hotel-style rooms, healthy locally sourced meals, and access to professional studios with sprung floors and pianos, a 160-seat black box theater, and exhibition space. Applications are accepted on a rolling basis through an inquiry form, with early inquiries encouraged to secure preferred dates. Artists at any career stage worldwide may apply, individually or as collaborative teams. There is no application fee and no artist stipend.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: The Factory on Willow Funded Artist in Residence
item.description:
Organisation: The Factory on Willow

Location: Manchester, New Hampshire, USA

Award: Furnished studio apartment with heat, hot water, electricity and internet, plus a USD 350 travel stipend and a USD 1600 stipend toward food and art supplies

Duration: 12 weeks (about 3 months)

Deadline: Rolling / undated

The Factory on Willow is a non-profit funded artist residency in Manchester, New Hampshire, housed in a 90,000 square foot mill building from 1904. The 12 week program supports amateur and emerging artists of any medium to develop their current projects and creative practice. Selected artists receive a furnished studio apartment with utilities and internet, a USD 350 travel stipend, and a USD 1600 stipend covering food and art supplies. The residency runs in three annual sessions (March to May, June to August, and September to November), with two artists selected per session. Each residency concludes with a public capstone showcase, and participants donate one work to the Factory's permanent collection. There is no application fee and applications are accepted on a rolling basis.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: CERF+ Emergency Relief Grants
item.description:
Organisation: CERF+ (The Artists Safety Net / Craft Emergency Relief Fund)

Location: United States and US Territories

Award: USD 3,000 one-time grant

Duration: One-time grant

Deadline: Rolling / undated

CERF+ provides Emergency Relief Grants of USD 3,000 to craft artists in the United States who have experienced a recent and substantially disruptive career-threatening emergency or disaster. Eligible applicants are individual craft artists aged 18 or older who have lived and worked in the US or US Territories for the past two years and who make original three-dimensional visual art objects by hand, including folk and traditional art forms. The grant is a one-time non-taxable gift with no reporting required on how funds are used. Applications are accepted on a rolling basis, though the program will not fund applicants if too much time has passed since the qualifying event. There is no application fee. Artists may receive one grant per emergency, no more than one per calendar year, and up to four grants over their lifetime.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: ACA Artist Response Fund (ARF) Emergency Relief Grant
item.description:
Organisation: Artist Communities Alliance

Location: United States (ACA member residencies)

Award: Up to USD 1,500

Duration: One-time emergency grant; applications processed within about one week

Deadline: Rolling / undated

The Artist Response Fund (ARF) is an emergency relief program from the Artist Communities Alliance (ACA) for artists who experience threats to their safety or mental health during a residency and must leave before completing their term. Qualifying situations include assault, harassment, or systemic harm where the artist has attempted to address the issue without success. Grants of up to USD 1,500 help cover relocation costs such as transportation home, temporary lodging, supplies or basic needs, and rent upon returning. Eligible applicants are individual artists currently at, or having attended within the past 12 months, an ACA Organizational Member residency. There is no application fee, and the fund operates on a rolling basis with applications typically processed within one week. Artists apply directly through an online form and can also contact ACA staff with questions.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Musicians Foundation Grants
item.description:
Organisation: Musicians Foundation

Location: United States (remote application)

Award: One-time emergency financial assistance; grant amount varies by need. Funds are issued as vendorized checks paid directly to billers (medical providers, landlords, utilities).

Duration: One-time grant

Deadline: Rolling / undated

The Musicians Foundation provides one-time financial assistance to professional musicians facing a specific time of need caused by an unexpected hardship such as a medical or dental situation, a natural disaster, or a housing crisis. Eligible applicants must be professional performers, composers/arrangers, or educators who have worked as professional musicians in the United States for five or more years, currently reside in the U.S., and have filed taxes as a musician for at least five years. The application has a rolling, always-open structure with a Pre-Application followed by a more detailed second part for those who qualify. Grants are issued as vendorized checks made payable directly to the biller and mailed to the applicant's home address. Processing typically takes between one and twelve weeks depending on volume.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Wassaic Project 2027 Summer Exhibition Open Call
item.description:
Organisation: The Wassaic Project

Location: Wassaic, New York, United States (in and around historic Wassaic and Maxon Mills).

Award: A modest honorarium for all accepted artists (part of the organization's effort toward W.A.G.E. certification). Artists creating site-specific installations are also eligible for an Exhibitions Fellowship and a no-fee residency of 1-4 weeks in April or May 2027.

Duration: The 2027 Summer Exhibition runs free and open to the public every weekend, 1 May to 12 September 2027. Optional fellowship residency of 1-4 weeks in April or May 2027.

Deadline: 10 Aug 2026

The Wassaic Project is holding its annual Open Call for the 2027 Summer Exhibition, for artists of all mediums, including 2D work, sculpture, video, new media, site-specific installation, performance, text, poems, essays, and publication-specific work. Selected work is showcased alongside a diverse range of pieces and performances in and around historic Wassaic and Maxon Mills; the 2027 Summer Exhibition will be free and open to the public every weekend from 1 May to 12 September 2027. In an effort toward W.A.G.E. certification, all accepted artists receive a modest honorarium to participate. Artists interested in creating a site-specific installation are also eligible for an Exhibitions Fellowship to help realize their work; Fellows are offered a no-fee residency for 1-4 weeks in April or May 2027 (artists interested in making site-specific work should still apply regardless of whether they are interested in or able to be in residence). The Wassaic Project cultivates and supports community for emerging and professional contemporary artists, writers, and other creatives, with a residency program housed in historic landmark buildings that hosts 10 artists each month in the heart of a rural community. APPLICATION: requires application forms, a CV, and an artwork/portfolio; submitted via SlideRoom. Application fee: USD 25 (no participation fee or additional fees). Deadline 10 August 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: dadalab: Extreme Heat Exhibition (Call for Artists)
item.description:
Organisation: dadalab

Location: Austin, Texas, United States (East Austin). Open to Austin-based BIPOC artists.

Award: USD 2,000 artist fee plus a supply stipend. Three artists selected.

Duration: Create original work for a two-day exhibition, 3-4 October 2026.

Deadline: Closed 03 Jul 2026

dadalab is seeking three Austin-based BIPOC artists to create original works exploring extreme heat through the lenses of science, public health and labor. The open call is medium-agnostic and welcomes work in any format. The goal is to bring awareness to environmental justice and the impact of extreme heat in Austin. AWARD: each selected artist receives a USD 2,000 artist fee plus a supply stipend. The resulting works are shown in a two-day exhibition on 3-4 October 2026 at dadalab in East Austin. ELIGIBILITY: open to Austin-based BIPOC artists working in any medium. Application deadline 3 July 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Helene Wurlitzer Foundation Residency
item.description:
Organisation: The Helene Wurlitzer Foundation of New Mexico

Location: Taos, New Mexico, USA

Award: Rent-free, utility-paid furnished housing (casita); no stipend (rare stipend support via the Rhonda Artist Stipend Fund); artists cover food and travel USD 30 application fee

Duration: 10 to 12 weeks (Sessions 1 and 3 are 12 weeks, Session 2 is 10 weeks)

Deadline: 18 Jan 2027

The Helene Wurlitzer Foundation of New Mexico runs one of the oldest artist residency programs in the United States, established in 1954, on a 15-acre campus in Taos, New Mexico. The residency is open to visual artists, literary artists, and music composers, including painters, sculptors, photographers, filmmakers, writers, poets, playwrights, screenwriters, and songwriters. Selected grantees receive rent-free and utility-paid housing in a furnished casita for a session of 10 to 12 weeks, with access to a Commons House offering WiFi, a library, and bicycles. No stipend is provided in most cases, and residents cover their own food and transportation. Applications are open to creative artists worldwide regardless of age, experience, or nationality, and the annual deadline is January 18 for residencies the following calendar year. There is a non-waivable USD 30 application fee.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Kala Fellowship Program
item.description:
Organisation: Kala Art Institute

Location: Berkeley, California, USA

Award: USD 3,000 stipend, 24/7 facility access for 1 to 9 months, 1 month of housing for artists outside the 9 Bay Area counties, a Kala class or workshop, and a group exhibition USD 25 application fee (free if applying December through January 15)

Duration: 1 to 9 months within the July 2026 to June 2027 fellowship period

Deadline: 01 Mar 2027

The Kala Fellowship Program supports international, national, and local artists producing innovative work in and across multiple media, with an emphasis on Kala's specialized printmaking, photography, and digital media equipment. The institute supports up to 6 artists per cycle with a USD 3,000 stipend, 24/7 access to its Berkeley facilities for 1 to 9 months, one month of housing for artists based outside the 9 Bay Area counties, a Kala class or workshop, and a culminating group show in the Kala Gallery. Applications are submitted through kala.slideroom.com and require a statement of interest, an artist CV, documentation of 5 to 10 recent works, and two references. Artists enrolled in a degree-granting program during the fellowship period are not eligible. The application opens in December with no fee through January 15, after which a USD 25 application fee applies through the March 1 deadline.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Roswell Artist-in-Residence Program
item.description:
Organisation: The RAiR Foundation

Location: Roswell, New Mexico, USA

Award: USD 1,100 per month stipend, plus USD 250 per month materials stipend from the Frederick Hammersley Foundation. Includes a private house and a studio with rent, utilities, repairs, and maintenance covered. USD 26.32 application fee

Duration: 12 months (one year)

Deadline: 15 Mar 2027

The Roswell Artist-in-Residence Program, established in 1967 and run by The RAiR Foundation, offers visual artists a year-long studio-based residency known as the Gift of Time. Each resident receives a private house and a studio on a fifty-acre complex, with rent, utilities, repairs, and maintenance covered by the program. Artists also receive a monthly stipend of USD 1,100 plus a USD 250 monthly materials stipend. The program is open to professional visual artists aged 21 and older working in painting, drawing, sculpture, printmaking, photography, video, digital media, and installation. It does not support performance art, writing, music, or production crafts. The application period for the next cycle runs from January 1 to March 15, 2027, and international applicants are accepted with visa support available.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Genesis Mission AI Fellowship 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Horizon Institute for Public Service (with Renaissance Philanthropy, SeedAI, and Fulcrum Science)

Location: United States. Fellows are forward deployed to US Department of Energy national labs (initial partners: Idaho National Laboratory, Brookhaven National Laboratory, Princeton Plasma Physics Laboratory). Remote or in-person; on-site travel at least once a quarter. Must be eligible to work in the US (no visa sponsorship; US green card holders eligible).

Award: Prorated total annual compensation of USD 200,000.

Duration: 9-12 months, starting fall 2026.

Deadline: 31 Jul 2026

The Horizon Institute for Public Service, Renaissance Philanthropy, SeedAI, and Fulcrum Science are partnering to launch a fellowship that embeds technical AI talent across the US Department of Energy national labs to advance the goals of the Genesis Mission, DOE's flagship science and technology initiative launched in late 2025. The Genesis Mission identifies 26 national science and technology challenges critical to US leadership and envisions a national platform connecting supercomputers, experimental facilities, AI systems, and unique datasets, with DOE's 17 national labs playing a key role. Genesis Mission AI Fellows are forward deployed to DOE national labs to bring frontier AI expertise to high-priority scientific challenges. As core members of the technical staff on a Genesis Mission project, fellows work alongside national lab scientists and partners and apply AI to national priorities, from building cutting-edge models of plasma dynamics in pursuit of controlled fusion to advancing state-of-the-art models for foundational materials discovery. Initial national lab partners include Idaho National Laboratory, Brookhaven National Laboratory, and the Princeton Plasma Physics Laboratory; additional partners may be announced later. COMPENSATION/DURATION: 9-12 months starting fall 2026, with a prorated total annual compensation of USD 200,000; fellows are expected to travel to their national lab site at least once a quarter but may work on-site if they prefer. ELIGIBILITY: competitive applicants should have at least 2 years of industry experience building AI models or agents (those with industry experience building AI applications or data infrastructure will also be considered); scientific domain expertise is not required, but any placement will involve some training and study in a specific scientific discipline or energy topic, supported by expert colleagues at the host institution. All fellows must be eligible to work in the United States for the duration of the placement; US employment visas cannot be sponsored, but US permanent residents (green card holders) are eligible. APPLICATION: initial form requires a CV or resume (max 2 pages) and short-answer questions on motivations and experiences (max 200 words); successful applicants complete interviews through partner organizations and national labs, and selected fellows are matched to partner labs by scientific domain and availability. The review uses evidence-based best practices including blind grading of application materials. TIMELINE: application deadline 31 July 2026; final fellow selection August-September 2026; expected start fall 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: The Studios at MASS MoCA Residencies
item.description:
Organisation: MASS MoCA (Massachusetts Museum of Contemporary Art)

Location: North Adams, Massachusetts, USA

Award: Provides private 24/7 studio space, furnished housing, and seven communal meals per week. Financial aid is available so artists requesting it typically pay between USD 0 and USD 450 per week, and fully funded fellowships cover all residency fees for up to four weeks. Artists who fully self-fund pay a subsidized rate of USD 650 per week.

Duration: 2 or 4 weeks

Deadline: 01 Nov 2026

The Studios at MASS MoCA is a year-round residency program on the museum's campus in North Adams, Massachusetts, hosting up to 10 artists at a time for stays of two or four weeks. It primarily welcomes visual artists across all media and writers in all literary disciplines, and applicants from around the world are encouraged to apply. Residents receive 24-hour access to a private furnished studio, lodging in nearby apartments, and several communal meals each week, plus museum membership benefits and access to The Clark Art Institute. The program offers need-based financial aid (artists typically pay between USD 0 and USD 450 per week) and fully funded fellowships that cover all residency fees for up to four weeks. General application deadlines fall on May 1 and November 1 each year, and the application is free to submit.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Rauschenberg Medical Emergency Grants
item.description:
Organisation: New York Foundation for the Arts (NYFA) / Robert Rauschenberg Foundation

Location: United States (remote application)

Award: One-time grants up to USD 5,000

Duration: One-time grant

Deadline: 17 Nov 2026

The Rauschenberg Medical Emergency Grants, administered by the New York Foundation for the Arts on behalf of the Robert Rauschenberg Foundation, provide one-time grants of up to USD 5,000 to help artists in financial need cover recent unexpected medical, dental, and mental health emergencies. The program is open to generative visual and media artists and choreographers working in visual arts, film, video, electronic and digital arts, and choreography who live anywhere in the United States, the District of Columbia, a Tribal Nation, or a U.S. Territory. Applicants must be artists in financial need, with adjusted gross income at or below USD 80,000 for an individual or USD 160,000 for joint filers, and must not have received a Rauschenberg Medical or Dancer Emergency Grant in the past five years. Eligible expenses include emergency medical bills, diagnostics, consultations, therapy, prescription medications, emergency dental care, and transportation to appointments. The grant does not cover lost wages, general living expenses, or care for anyone other than the artist. Applications are accepted during defined cycles, with the current cycle running roughly from late October to mid November.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Farm, Flock and Fiber Artist in Residence
item.description:
Organisation: A Wrinkle in Thyme Farm

Location: Sumner, Maine, USA

Award: Modest monthly stipend (around USD 400/month) in exchange for roughly 15 to 20 hours/week of farm work, plus free rustic private housing (solar power, wood stove), shared kitchen, bath and WiFi, community meals and farm-grown food, dedicated solo studio time, and weekly collaboration with resident fiber artist Marty Elkin. USD 25 non-refundable application fee

Duration: Six months beginning in May, with an optional two additional months for lambing and early lamb care

Deadline: 31 Dec 2026

A Wrinkle in Thyme Farm is a small, women-owned diversified farm in Sumner, Maine that offers a six-month live-in fiber arts residency starting in May. The resident exchanges an average of 15 to 20 hours per week of farm work for a modest monthly stipend, free private rustic housing, community meals, farm-grown food, and access to the fiber studio. Residents receive solo studio time plus one day a week of learning and collaboration with fiber artist Marty Elkin, working with home-grown Maine wool through felting, dyeing, knitting and spinning. The program also includes training in flock management, animal husbandry and lamb care, with the option to add two months for lambing season. It is designed for emerging fiber artists who are single females able to lift 50 pounds and work around a farm. Applications open in November with a December 31 deadline and a non-refundable USD 25 application fee.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: NEIU Art + Design Studio Art Residency 2026-2027
item.description:
Organisation: Northeastern Illinois University (NEIU), Art + Design Department

Location: Salme Harju Steinberg Fine Arts Center, Northeastern Illinois University, Chicago, Illinois, United States. In-person; semester-long access to NEIU facilities.

Award: USD 800 honorarium, plus a semester of full access to NEIU Art + Design facilities specific to the project (Art + Tech, Ceramics, Drawing, Painting, Photography, Printmaking, Sculpture); supplies can be requested with guidance and approval from Studio Area personnel. Visiting Artists may also exhibit in a group exhibition in the NEIU Fine Arts Center Gallery.

Duration: One semester. Residency Session 1: 25 January - 14 May; Session 2: 30 August - 11 December.

Deadline: 09 Aug 2026

The NEIU Art + Design Studio Art Residency strives to create visible connections between Northeastern Illinois University's diverse population of art and design students and working artists who embody these identities, providing insights and inspiration through a dynamic exchange of ideas and techniques. Visiting Artists should demonstrate a commitment to their artistic practice and an interest in engaging with NEIU's students and creative community. Each Visiting Artist is awarded an honorarium and has full access for a semester to the NEIU Art + Design facilities specific to their project; over the residency they showcase their work in a public academic forum, lead a hands-on workshop, and participate in a Portfolio Review Day for NEIU's Art + Design students. FACILITIES (in the Salme Harju Steinberg Fine Arts Center) include a Design Studio with the largest collection of Riso drums at any Chicago university, a Fab Lab (FDM and resin 3D printers, large-format CNC router, vinyl cutters, laser cutters), a Ceramic Studio (throwing wheels, glaze room, electric and gas kilns, raku area), a Letterpress Studio (two Vandercook presses, metal and wood type), a Sculpture Studio (wood shop, clay modeling and spray painting areas), a Photography Studio (analog and digital, plus a lighting studio), and fully equipped Painting, Drawing, and Printmaking studios. BENEFITS: USD 800 honorarium; facility access based on project focus (Art + Tech, Ceramics, Drawing, Painting, Photography, Printmaking, Sculpture), with supplies requestable subject to approval; and the opportunity to exhibit in a group exhibition in the NEIU Fine Arts Center Gallery. PARTICIPATION: lead a 2-hour hands-on workshop for 15-20 students on a topic relevant to the project; give an hour-long artist talk with Q&A; and serve as a guest panelist for a day-long portfolio review. KEY DATES: open call 2 June - 9 August; emailed acceptances in October; Residency Session 1 runs 25 January - 14 May and Session 2 runs 30 August - 11 December. Apply via the JotForm application. Deadline 9 August 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: 100 Voices of Florida Film Grant
item.description:
Organisation: 100 Voices of Florida

Location: Florida, United States

Award: Up to USD 5,000 (unrestricted) USD 25 application fee with a Florida connection, USD 35 without

Duration: One-time grant

Deadline: 20 Sep 2026

100 Voices of Florida is a grant for filmmakers and screenwriters that awards up to USD 5,000 in unrestricted funds, which winners can use for finishing funds, festival submission fees, cast and crew reimbursements, production, or anything else. Applicants submit either a short film (under twenty minutes) or a script (twenty pages or less); films in other languages must include accurate English subtitles, and scripts must be in English. The grant is open to writers, directors, and producers who are at least 18 years old, and creative teams may submit, though only one entrant receives the award. A panel of judges with professional filmmaking experience evaluates entries, and the winner also receives a meeting with an industry professional. The deadline for the current cycle is September 20, 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Open Interval: An Artist-Scientist Exploration of Turbulence
item.description:
Organisation: Simons Foundation

Location: United States (trio must be regionally co-located)

Award: USD 30,000 per awarded trio

Duration: April to December 2027 (several months of collaboration)

Deadline: 21 Oct 2026

Open Interval is a Simons Foundation program that supports trios consisting of an artist, a scientist, and a representative from a 501(c)(3) arts organization to collaborate around a shared scientific theme. The current cycle centers on the theme of turbulence, a phenomenon at the edge of chaos and beauty. Awarded trios receive USD 30,000 to support a sustained period of thought partnership and exploration rather than the production of finished works or exhibitions. The program runs from April to December 2027 with a touchstone meeting in May 2027 and possible cohort convenings. Trio members must be regionally co-located anywhere in the United States so they can collaborate in person. The scientist should hold a Ph.D. and be conducting basic research, with priority given to fields such as astrophysics, neuroscience, quantum physics, biology, or mathematics. The application portal opens September 9, 2026, and all three trio members must submit for the application to be complete.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Dedalus Foundation Senior Fellowship 2027
item.description:
Organisation: The Dedalus Foundation

Location: United States (remote, project-based)

Award: Stipend up to USD 30,000

Duration: Up to one year

Deadline: 15 Sep 2026

The Dedalus Foundation, established by artist Robert Motherwell, awards a Senior Fellowship supporting critical and historical studies related to painting, sculpture, and allied arts of the twentieth century. The fellowship is open to art historians, critics, curators, and other writers and scholars who have demonstrated their abilities through previous accomplishments. Awards provide a stipend of up to USD 30,000 for projects lasting up to one year, with the amount varying by project needs. Applicants must be U.S. citizens and may not be currently pursuing academic degrees. Applications are submitted online and reference letters through a separate form. A distinguished scholar jury selects recipients, with awards announced by mid-December.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Jentel Artist Residency
item.description:
Organisation: Jentel Foundation

Location: Banner, Wyoming, USA

Award: USD 100 weekly stipend (about USD 300 total over three weeks), plus private bedroom, separate studio space, and shared living and kitchen facilities provided at no cost USD 30 application fee

Duration: Approximately 3 weeks (arrive on the 15th, depart the morning of the 7th of the following month)

Deadline: 15 Sep 2026

The Jentel Artist Residency offers approximately three-week residencies in the foothills of the Bighorn Mountains near Banner, Wyoming, giving artists, writers, and composers dedicated time for creative work. Sessions run from mid-January through early December, hosting six residents at a time. It is open to visual artists in any medium, writers of fiction, creative nonfiction, or poetry, and composers across musical genres. Residents receive a private bedroom, a separate studio, shared living and kitchen space, and a USD 100 weekly stipend to help cover food and personal expenses. Applicants must be at least 25 years old and be U.S. citizens or foreign nationals whose primary residence is in the U.S. during the application year, and may not be enrolled full-time in a degree program. There is a USD 30 non-refundable application fee, with deadlines of September 15 for Winter and Spring sessions and January 15 for Summer and Fall sessions.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Illuminations Grant for Black Trans Women Visual Artists
item.description:
Organisation: Queer|Art

Location: United States; open to self-identified Black trans women and trans femmes working in visual art and based in the US.

Award: USD 10,000 to one winning artist, plus USD 1,250 each to four distinguished finalists. Winners and finalists also receive additional professional development resources and guidance, and each judge conducts a studio visit with the winning artist.

Duration: One-time award

Deadline: Closed 02 Jul 2026

Now in its 7th year, the Illuminations Grant for Black Trans Women Visual Artists is an annual USD 10,000 grant from Queer|Art that provides critical support to Black trans women whose work has often been under-recognized in the visual art field. To further recognize finalists for their artistic achievements, Queer|Art also provides a USD 1,250 award to four distinguished finalists. Winning artists and finalists receive additional professional development resources and further guidance to bolster their creative development. The grant was developed and named in partnership with Mariette Pathy Allen, Aaryn Lang, and Serena Jara, and is made possible entirely through support from visual artist Mariette Pathy Allen, whose photographic work over the last forty years has focused on expanding cultural consciousness around gender and transformation. It is administered through Queer|Art with a rotating panel of judges, each of whom conducts a studio visit with the winning artist as part of the award's focus on creative and professional development; judges for the 2026 cycle include Jordyn Jay, Isis Awad, and Utē Petit. ELIGIBILITY: qualified artists must be self-identified Black trans women and trans femmes working in visual art and based in the United States. Deadline: July 2, 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: XENO Prize for Artists' Books
item.description:
Organisation: Franklin Furnace

Location: United States.

Award: USD 5,000 to one artist to publish one book on the topic of book banning and/or burning, in an edition of at least 100 copies.

Duration: One-time prize

Deadline: Closed 04 Jul 2026

In pursuit of its mission to present, preserve, interpret, educate, and advocate on behalf of avant-garde art, especially forms that may be vulnerable due to institutional neglect, cultural bias, their ephemeral nature, or politically unpopular content, Franklin Furnace offers the annual XENO Prizes for Artists' Books. One artist will receive USD 5,000 to publish one book on the topic of book banning and/or burning in an edition of at least 100 copies. Deadline: July 4, 2026.

item.title: Loghaven Artist Residency
item.description:
Organisation: Loghaven Artist Residency

Location: Knoxville, Tennessee, USA

Award: USD 850 weekly living stipend, travel subsidy USD 400 to 800, up to USD 200 materials shipping reimbursement, plus room and board, housing in historic log cabins, and studio space USD 25 non-refundable application fee (fee waiver available on request)

Duration: 2 to 6 weeks depending on session

Deadline: 15 Jul 2026

Loghaven Artist Residency offers practicing artists time, space, and resources on ninety wooded acres in Knoxville, Tennessee. Fellows stay in five rehabilitated historic log cabins with access to purpose-built studio space. Each Fellow receives an USD 850 weekly living stipend, a travel subsidy on a sliding scale from USD 400 to 800, up to USD 200 in materials shipping reimbursement, and covered room and board. Residencies run from 2 to 6 weeks across sessions scheduled throughout 2027. The program is open to artists at any career stage working in architecture, dance, interdisciplinary art, music composition, theater, visual art, and writing, including collaborative groups of up to nine people. Applicants must be at least 21, live more than 120 miles from Knoxville, and be eligible to work legally in the US; international artists and degree-seeking students are not eligible.

item.title: The Center for Fiction / Susan Kamil Emerging Writer Fellowship
item.description:
Organisation: The Center for Fiction

Location: New York City, USA

Award: USD 5,000 grant plus writers studio space, mentorship, agent introductions, and community programming

Duration: One year (the fellowship year)

Deadline: 31 Jul 2026

The Center for Fiction / Susan Kamil Emerging Writer Fellowship annually supports nine early-career, New York City-based fiction writers at a pivotal point in their careers. Each fellow receives a 5,000 USD grant, dedicated space and time to write in the Writers Studio, personalized editorial mentorship, opportunities to meet literary agents, and community programming. Applicants must be current residents of one of the five boroughs and must remain in New York City for the entire fellowship year. An emerging writer is defined as someone who has not yet had a novel or short story collection published by a major or independent publisher and who is not currently under contract for a work of fiction; students enrolled in a degree-granting program during the fellowship year are not eligible. The 2027 fellowship application window opens July 1, 2026 and closes July 31, 2026.

item.title: MSB Interpretive Panels: Public Art Commission (Creative Philadelphia)
item.description:
Organisation: Creative Philadelphia (Office of Arts, Culture and the Creative Economy)

Location: Municipal Services Building, Center City, Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, United States. Philadelphia-based artists strongly encouraged; artists from outside the region considered. Multiple in-person site visits required (travel costs covered within the project budget).

Award: Total project budget of USD 250,000, which must cover: artist fees (18% design fee), design and engineering, fabrication and installation, structural footings/anchoring, travel and site visits, AR development (software, hosting, content production), insurance (1%), community engagement participation, and a service warranty keeping content compatible with new mobile OS versions for a minimum of 10 years. No application fees.

Duration: Permanent public artwork commission; two-part selection process.

Deadline: 12 Jul 2026

Creative Philadelphia announces a call to commission a series of permanent interpretive panels with integrated augmented reality (AR) components at the Municipal Services Building in Center City Philadelphia. The commission is part of a broader initiative that includes an upcoming public sculpture of Dr. Sadie T.M. Alexander by artist Vinnie Bagwell. Inspired by Alexander's groundbreaking legacy in public service, these interpretive panels will celebrate the City of Philadelphia's municipal employees (past, present, and future) and provide deeper context for understanding Dr. Alexander's impact; they will be located in front of the building's landscaped areas, flanking the upcoming sculpture, and are intended to complement and support the overall site experience. SCOPE: an artist team will design and fabricate four (4) permanent, sculptural interpretive panels incorporating augmented reality (AR) elements to expand storytelling beyond the physical surface of the artwork. ELIGIBILITY: all interested artists are invited to apply; Philadelphia-based artists are strongly encouraged, though artists from outside the region will be considered; multiple in-person site visits are required and associated travel costs must be covered within the project budget. SELECTION: a two-part process: Part 1 submission of qualifications; Part 2 finalist proposals and interviews. Up to three (3) finalists are invited to submit short concept proposals and attend an interview; at no point should artists submit design proposals. BUDGET: USD 250,000 total (see amount for the required cost breakdown). An Artist Informational Meeting is held via Zoom on 30 June, 6:00-7:00pm. Application questions: paige.phillips@phila.gov. Apply via Submittable. Deadline 12 July 2026 (submissions close Monday 13 July 2026, 6:00 AM).

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: GovAI U.S. AI Policy Program
item.description:
Organisation: Centre for the Governance of AI (GovAI)

Location: Washington, D.C., USA (hybrid; remote participation available)

Award: Free to participate; in-person attendees provided dinner (value kept at or below USD 20 per session)

Duration: 12-week part-time program, early September to early December 2026 (approximately 5 hours per week)

Deadline: Closed 05 Jul 2026

The GovAI U.S. AI Policy Program is a bipartisan, part-time, 12-week program designed to accelerate or launch careers in U.S. AI policy. Open to U.S. policy professionals across government and think tanks, it builds a technically-informed understanding of the AI policy landscape through guided discussion sessions, with an emphasis on general-purpose AI. Topics include how general-purpose AI systems are developed; inputs to AI development such as energy, chips, and data centers; AI's effects on productivity, scientific discovery, and economic growth; national security and great power competition; corporate self-governance; AI regulation in other jurisdictions; and federal and state AI regulatory proposals in the U.S. Participants also have weekly opportunities to question world-leading experts; prior guest speakers have included White House OSTP staffers, former frontier-lab employees, and people who helped draft U.S. state- and federal-level AI policy proposals. The program runs early September to early December 2026 (break for Thanksgiving week) with a roughly 5-hour weekly commitment: two hours of synchronous evening sessions (EST), held in-person at GovAI's D.C. office near DuPont Circle or remotely with cameras on, plus three hours of independent reading and podcasts from a free syllabus. To list completion on a resume, participants must attend 9 of 12 sessions, write a short policy memo or op-ed, and pass an interview-based exam. Eligible participants include executive and legislative branch staff (fellows, interns, detailees, military personnel, national laboratory personnel) and think-tank policy researchers and affiliates, across a range of seniority levels, with preference for the D.C. metropolitan area; participants need not currently cover AI but should aspire to a career in U.S. tech or national security policy. Selection has two stages: an online application with six biographical questions, a resume, two verbal reasoning questions, and an approximately 7-minute video; followed by a 30-minute interview in July, with final decisions in early August.

Open call details

item.title: Technology Fellow: Digital Safety and Security
item.description:
Organisation: Ford Foundation

Location: New York, New York, USA

Award: USD 166,000 to 170,000 annual salary, plus comprehensive benefits and retirement contributions of up to 13%

Duration: Two-year term-limited contract

Deadline: Closed 06 Jul 2026

The Ford Foundation seeks a two-year Technology Fellow for Digital Safety and Security to expand the foundation's ability to support grantee digital safety and security. Civil society organizations face targeted information security threats yet many lack the resources and training to maintain robust digital security. Reporting to the Director, Office of the Vice President for U.S. Programs, the Fellow will identify field-building opportunities in the public-interest digital safety and security field, work directly with program officers across US and global teams to integrate digital security capacity into Ford's technical assistance programs, provide strategic support to high-risk grantees, and assess strategic gaps in the ecosystem for safety and security providers. The Technology Fellows Program is administered by Ford's Technology and Society Program and is designed to increase sociotechnical capacity within the Foundation. Requirements include a minimum of 7 years' professional experience with media, technology, advocacy or policy organizations; a baccalaureate degree or equivalent experience; sophisticated knowledge of existing and emerging technologies with a focus on surveillance technologies and organizational digital safety and security; and experience designing, implementing or supporting digital safety and security solutions in a complex global setting. Hybrid role based in New York (three days per week in office) with approximately 10 to 20 percent domestic and international travel. Not eligible for visa sponsorship; candidates must be authorized to work in the United States. Submit both a resume and a cover letter to be considered.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Technology Fellow: AI and Philanthropy
item.description:
Organisation: Ford Foundation

Location: New York, New York, USA

Award: USD 166,000 to 170,000 annual salary, plus comprehensive benefits and retirement contributions of up to 13%

Duration: Two-year term-limited contract

Deadline: Closed 06 Jul 2026

The Ford Foundation seeks a two-year Technology Fellow for AI and Philanthropy, an internally-facing role focused on cross-programmatic learning and advisory support. Reporting to the Director, Technology and Society, the AI Tech Fellow will support Foundation staff in building and adapting internal frameworks for evaluating AI use in ways that prioritize mission and strategy and address ethical, sociotechnical, and operational considerations. Responsibilities include designing and curating hands-on trainings, workshops, and learning sessions to help staff build practical familiarity with AI tools and critically assess their value and efficacy; creating accessible documentation and guides on mission-aligned AI experimentation; curating sociotechnical conversations on how philanthropy and the nonprofit sector are experimenting with AI in ways that respect privacy, safety, and equity; monitoring emerging global AI developments and providing timely briefings to program teams; conducting landscape research on AI initiatives in the philanthropy and social justice sectors; and building relationships with AI researchers, practitioners, grantees and partners working on mission-enabling AI. The Technology Fellows Program is administered by Ford's Technology and Society Program and is designed to increase sociotechnical capacity within the Foundation. Requirements include a minimum of 7 years working within public-interest, government, civil society, or commercial technology sectors; a baccalaureate degree or equivalent experience; demonstrated sociotechnical expertise in artificial intelligence and related technologies including machine learning systems and generative AI and their societal implications; and experience designing, implementing or supporting AI applications and tools in a complex global setting. Hybrid role based in New York (three days per week in office). Not eligible for visa sponsorship; candidates must be authorized to work in the United States. Submit both a resume and a cover letter to be considered.

Open call details

item.title: Art Shanty Projects 2027: Call for Performances + Art Actions
item.description:
Organisation: Art Shanty Projects

Location: Bde Uman (Lake Harriet), Minneapolis, Minnesota, United States. On-ice program plus several mandatory in-person meetings in Minneapolis; in-person participation required.

Award: Sliding-scale stipend up to USD 1,000 (request an amount that fits the project: roughly USD 1,000 for full-day/most-ambitious activities, USD 500 for half-day, USD 250 for short or one-off/solo/low-production activities). Plus direct production support, discounted materials and resource sharing, a shared warming shanty, a hot meal, snacks, hot beverages, hand/toe warmers, one parking space per project, on-site medic, and ASL interpreter/audio describer for the public. No application fees.

Duration: On-ice program every Saturday and Sunday, 10am-4pm, 16 January to 7 February 2027, with production meetings and mandatory check-ins from September 2026 onward.

Deadline: 09 Jul 2026

Art Shanty Projects seeks winter enthusiasts and creative adventurers to create performances and art actions for its 2027 Program on Bde Uman (Lake Harriet) in Minneapolis. It invites music-makers, poets, builders, scientists, dancers, puppeteers, writers, dreamers, activists, outdoors-people, conversationalists, craftspeople, storytellers, and more to propose new and innovative ways to engage artistically with the public in the social context of a village on a frozen lake (or winter 'beach', as climate change may necessitate). ASP has an expansive notion of what constitutes a performance or art action, having welcomed dance, creative sporting events, happenings, carnival games, music, roving dance, temporary sculpture, puppet shows, artful haircuts, advice booths, climate actions, and more. NOTE: this call is for PERFORMANCES + ART ACTIONS only (a separate call exists for shanty artists). Since 2004, Art Shanty Projects has created joyful winter art experiences inspired by pop-up ice fishing villages, using the frozen lake as a public platform for a temporary creative community with tens of thousands of annual visitors. ASP is deeply committed to social, racial, gender, and economic justice and to accessibility (embracing the spirit of the ADA). ELIGIBILITY: everyone is eligible (artists at all career stages); applicants need not have experience with everything asked, but should show eagerness to engage, develop new skills, and stay open to new perspectives. Projects that involve selling anything or the exchange of legal currency will not be accepted. COMPENSATION: sliding-scale stipend up to USD 1,000 plus extensive production and logistical support. PROGRAM: on-ice every Saturday and Sunday, 10am-4pm, 16 January to 7 February 2027; selected artists notified mid-August 2026, with mandatory in-person meetings (at least one team member) starting 10 September 2026. Apply via the Google form (preferred) or the no-Google-account alternative. Contact Erin Lavelle, Artistic Director: program@artshantyprojects.org. Deadline for proposal submissions: 9 July 2026, 11:59pm CST.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Justice + Joy Summer Research Fellowship 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Justice + Joy

Location: United States; fully virtual program. Capstone showcase tentatively in person (mid-September 2026), with travel and lodging covered.

Award: USD 2,000 stipend paid upon successful program completion, plus skills-based training, guest speaker sessions, and peer community. All travel and lodging for the in-person capstone are covered.

Duration: Seven-week virtual program, 9 July to 20 August 2026, with a capstone showcase mid-September 2026. Weekly Wednesday sessions 5:00-6:30 PM ET plus approximately 2-4 hours of independent work per week.

Deadline: 09 Jul 2026

The Justice + Joy Research Fellowship is a seven-week virtual summer program for girls, young women, and gender-expansive young people of color (ages 18-26) in the United States who want to explore issues impacting their lives, communities, and futures. Fellows identify a topic they care about, such as mental health, education, reproductive justice, immigration, AI and technology, gender-based violence, beauty standards, young parenthood, or another issue affecting their communities, and develop an original research project grounded in their experiences, questions, and ideas. Throughout the summer, fellows participate in weekly virtual sessions, engage with guest speakers, build community with peers from across the country, and receive mentorship and support as they develop their research. The fellowship culminates in a capstone showcase where fellows present their work to the Justice + Joy community, including organizational leaders, partners, and supporters. No prior research experience is required; the program seeks young people who are curious, committed to learning, and interested in using research as a tool for understanding, advocacy, and change. COMMITMENT: weekly virtual sessions on Wednesdays 5:00-6:30 PM ET (9 July - 20 August 2026); approximately 2-4 hours of independent work per week; required orientation on Wednesday 9 July; development of an individual research proposal or conceptual framework; presentation at the capstone showcase (tentatively in person, mid-September 2026, with all travel and lodging covered). WHAT YOU GET: a USD 2,000 stipend paid upon successful completion; skills-based training in research methods, social justice frameworks, and community advocacy; guest speaker sessions; a peer community; and a real audience for the research (the Justice + Joy board and funders). Apply via the Google Form. Note: an explicit application deadline is not stated; the required orientation and program start is 9 July 2026, so apply before then.

Open call details

item.title: Windgate Artist-in-Residence Fall 2026 (Arkansas Tech University)
item.description:
Organisation: Arkansas Tech University (supported by the Windgate Charitable Foundation)

Location: Russellville, Arkansas, United States (Russellville campus, in the Arkansas River Valley, about one hour from Little Rock). In-person, full-time residency.

Award: USD 30,000 stipend (minus fringes) plus USD 18,000 for supplies. Housing provided (a 2-bed, 2-bath campus apartment) and a large, climate-controlled, dedicated off-campus studio space.

Duration: Five-month residency over the Fall academic term 2026.

Deadline: Closed 03 Jul 2026

The Artist-in-Residence (AIR) program at Arkansas Tech University is an immersive experience within an art and educational community, intended to advance the careers of practicing artists, present residents as role models for the ATU community, and strengthen public awareness of the significance of making art and its effect on the surrounding community. The residency takes place over a five-month period, giving the artist time and space to focus on the creative process. For Fall 2026, the program will select an artist to create a public artwork that enhances the ATU campus; both two-dimensional and three-dimensional proposals will be considered. The program is supported by a generous grant from the Windgate Charitable Foundation. The Department of Art at ATU offers programs in Fine Art, Graphic Design, Game and Interactive Media Design, and Art Education, with a facility in Norman Hall featuring a professional gallery, computer lab, lecture hall, and studios for painting, drawing, printmaking, sculpture, ceramics, and graphic design. RESIDENCY DETAILS & EXPECTATIONS: residents must be artists with significant exhibition records, strong communication skills, sustained studio practice, and a desire to engage with the larger campus community; involvement may include guest lecturing, public talks, mentoring students, open studios, and/or collaborating with faculty and students. The resident sometimes teaches one course in 2D Design, 3D Design, or Introduction to Drawing during their stay when enrollment allows. Artists are expected to commit as a full-time resident for the Fall academic term 2026. BENEFITS: USD 30,000 stipend (minus fringes), USD 18,000 for supplies, a 2-bed/2-bath campus apartment, and a large climate-controlled off-campus studio. Deadline 3 July 2026.

Open call details

item.title: Circuit Breakers '26: Tech Worker Conference - Call for Proposals (New York City)
item.description:
Organisation: Tech Workers Coalition

Location: New York City, USA. In-person conference 17-18 October 2026. Open to workers across the tech sector, from software engineers and salespeople to warehouse workers, ride-share drivers and contractors.

Award: No funding. This is a free call for talk/session proposals at a volunteer-organised tech labour conference, not a grant. No submission fee.

Duration: Two-day conference, 17-18 October 2026.

Deadline: 15 Jul 2026

Circuit Breakers '26 is a tech worker conference organised by volunteers from across the tech labour movement, bringing together workers, organisers and activists in the tech industry to learn, build community and strategise. Tech Workers Coalition defines tech workers broadly, from software engineers and salespeople to warehouse workers and ride-share drivers. The conference centres rank-and-file organising, with workers sharing stories, running skills trainings and leading debates. The open call for proposals welcomes sessions on all aspects of labour organising in tech, with particular interest in three areas: Labor Organizing Against Imperialism; Tech Workers Taking on Technology (layoffs, surveillance, worker control over AI and automation); and Bold Strategy (solidarity, membership structures, return-to-office actions, cross-border organising). Sessions can be proposed via a short form (about 10 minutes). New York City, 17-18 October 2026. This is not a grant and carries no funding; it is a free call for conference talks. NOTE: the call-for-proposals deadline was not yet published at time of listing; confirm the submission deadline and form link on the Circuit Breakers page.

item.title: WSW Artist's Book Residency Grant
item.description:
Organisation: Women's Studio Workshop (WSW)

Location: Rosendale, NY, USA; in-person residency.

Award: $350/week stipend; up to $1,000 for materials; up to $250 for travel within the Continental US; free onsite housing; 24/7 studio access. No application fee.

Duration: 8-10 weeks; residency runs September through June, one year after application.

Deadline: 15 Nov 2026

Women's Studio Workshop (WSW) offers an eight- to ten-week residency for artists to produce a limited-edition artist's book. Working intensively in WSW's studios in Rosendale, NY, selected artists print and bind their own books; edition size must be between 50 and 100 copies. AMOUNT: $350/week stipend; up to $1,000 for materials; up to $250 for travel within the Continental US; free onsite housing; 24/7 studio access. WSW provides technical advice, training on new equipment, techniques, and materials, and production assistance. SALES CONTRACT: 20% of the edition goes to the artist; 20% (at least nine copies) stays with WSW for archival and institutional sales; 60% goes to general distribution and sale. If the artist sells through WSW, WSW takes a 30% commission plus 20% for marketing; the artist receives a 50% commission, paid annually around 30 June. WSW holds the right to sell distribution copies at a 20% discount to standing-order and repository institutions. WSW's books are held in over 450 collections worldwide, including the Library of Congress, Yale University, and Vassar College. An outside jury of artists and curators selects recipients. INDIGENOUS ARTISTS: applicants may check the appropriate box on the Submittable form to be considered for the BIMA Artist's Book Residency Grant for Indigenous Artists. APPLICATION MATERIALS (upload via Submittable): current resume; brief project description including media required (200-word maximum); materials budget form (Google Sheets, Excel, or PDF); up to ten images of recent work; image script (title, medium, dimensions, date). MAILED MATERIALS (to WSW at the address below): a physical book mock-up demonstrating how the book will be bound or function, actual size, with at least 1-2 fully realised spreads; and the shipping form (download and open the PDF locally). Mail to: Artist's Book Application, Women's Studio Workshop, PO Box 489, Rosendale, NY 12472. Applications open 1 August 2026; deadline 15 November 2026, 11:59pm EST; notification March 2027; residency runs September through June, one year after application. Apply via Submittable at https://wsworkshop.org/residencies/artists-book-residency-grant/.

Open call details

item.title: AI Research Fellow / Senior AI Policy Researcher (Utah Office of AI Policy)
item.description:
Organisation: Utah Department of Commerce, Office of Artificial Intelligence Policy

Location: Salt Lake City, Utah, United States. Hybrid telework may be available but regular in-person work is required, and in-state work rules require all work to be performed while physically within Utah. No visa sponsorship.

Award: Salary: USD 33.13 to 54.01 hourly wage. Full-time, time-limited position. Job classification Research Consultant III (#16048); may underfill as Research Consultant II (#16058) with a reduced salary range.

Duration: Full-time, time-limited (Schedule Code TL).

Deadline: Closed 24 Jun 2026

The Utah Department of Commerce, Office of Artificial Intelligence Policy, is seeking an AI Research Fellow to help drive technical, scientific, and policy research for Utah's AI Learning Lab. This is an applied research and policy-translation role whose work should directly inform regulatory decisions, legislative recommendations, regulatory mitigation agreements, and stakeholder strategy. The role is technical and relies heavily on statistical, machine learning, computer science, and empirical research skills, while also requiring strong judgment, clear writing, and the ability to operate in a small multidisciplinary team alongside colleagues with policy, business, regulatory, and legal expertise. The Office of Artificial Intelligence Policy was created in May 2024 to systematically develop sensible AI policy across major impact areas such as healthcare, education, finance, transportation, and consumer technology. Central to the Office is the AI Learning Lab, where industry experts, academics, regulators, and policymakers work toward appropriate regulatory responses to emerging AI applications; the Office also has authority to create regulatory mitigation agreements that let responsible AI businesses test products in Utah under structured conditions while the state gathers evidence. KEY RESPONSIBILITIES: independently scope and execute research projects on emerging AI policy questions (defining the question, identifying technical and empirical evidence, interviewing experts, evaluating tradeoffs, and producing written recommendations); research the technical aspects, capabilities, limitations, and societal impacts of AI technologies; evaluate evidence and forecasts on the impact of human-AI interactions on health, economic activity, education, consumer welfare, and public institutions; draft executive policy memos, legislative briefings, technical explainers, risk assessments, and regulatory mitigation analyses; interface with business leaders, technologists, researchers, regulators, legislators, and lawyers; and advise on the development of regulatory and legal standards. MINIMUM QUALIFICATIONS: deep technical knowledge of statistical methods, machine learning, and AI systems; strong ability to translate technical and empirical evidence into practical policy options for nontechnical decision-makers; familiarity with human-subject research, qualitative and quantitative methods, medical or health sciences, social sciences, human-computer interaction, or related empirical fields; excellent problem-solving, reliability, and independent judgment; and strong technical communication across policy, legal, regulatory, business, and technical audiences. PREFERRED: experience implementing, evaluating, or deploying AI systems (business, healthcare, government, or other applied settings); expertise in public policy, law, regulatory design, or government decision-making; ability to evaluate technical claims, research methods, and evidence quality; specific healthcare experience; and experience supporting AI deployment in regulated environments. A criminal history check is required. Recruiter: Tehra Gorski, tgorski@utah.gov, 801-530-6030. Application deadline 24 June 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: NEW INC Year 13 Mentor Open Call
item.description:
Organisation: NEW INC (New Museum Incubator)

Location: New York, NY, USA; primarily in-person at 250 Hudson St, lower Manhattan. Some hybrid participation possible.

Award: Dedicated Mentors: $500 honorarium. Office Hours Mentors: $300 honorarium. All mentors also receive a one-year All Access Plus membership to the New Museum. No application fee.

Duration: Year 13 program (September 2026 onward); average 4-8 hours of engagement per week.

Deadline: 08 Jul 2026

NEW INC, the New Museum's cultural incubator, is seeking mentors for Year 13 of its program supporting creative practitioners at the intersection of art, design, and technology. This is a call for mentors (creative and business leaders), not for program members. TWO MENTOR TYPES: Dedicated Mentors ($500 honorarium) are matched one-on-one with a member and commit to ongoing support throughout the year; Office Hours Mentors ($300 honorarium) offer one-on-one sessions to the broader community on an as-needed basis. All mentors receive a one-year All Access Plus membership to the New Museum, invitations to special events including the annual DEMO Festival, and access to community-only workshops, talks, and social events. YEAR 13 PROGRAM TRACKS mentors may support: Art & Code; Creative Science; Extended Realities; Launchpad; Social Architecture. KEY DATES: Mentor Open Call closes 8 July 2026; Mentor Open House (virtual info session) 17 June 2026, 12-1pm ET (register via the site); mandatory Dedicated Mentor matchmaking ranking session 11 September 2026; mandatory Dedicated Mentor matchmaking event 18 September 2026; Dedicated Mentor matches announced 2 October 2026. Most NEW INC activities take place Monday-Friday, 10am-6pm ET. Apply at https://www.newmuseum.org/new-inc/open-call/.

item.title: Fulbright-Iméra Mediterranean Studies Award 2027-2028 (Aix-Marseille, US Faculty)
item.description:
Organisation: Iméra – Aix Marseille Université, in partnership with the Fulbright Commission

Location: Aix-Marseille, France (campus residency)

Award: €2,400 (~USD 2,600) monthly stipend; fully covered accommodation (valued USD 788–1,966/month); €1,200 (~USD 1,300) travel allowance. No application fee.

Duration: 5 or 10 months (September 2027 – June 2028, flexible start/end dates).

Deadline: 15 Sep 2026

The Fulbright-Iméra Mediterranean Studies Award supports US faculty to conduct research at the Iméra Institute for Advanced Study at Aix Marseille Université. ELIGIBILITY: mid-career, senior, or community college faculty affiliated with a US institution of higher education; US citizens residing outside France (permanent residents of France are ineligible); any discipline relating to Mediterranean studies; English sufficient (French desirable). 1 or 2 grants awarded. PROVIDED: €2,400/month stipend; fully equipped apartment with linens; office in co-working space; visa fees waived; academic event organisation expenses covered; school placement assistance for dependents. DURATION: 5 or 10 months (September 2027 – June 2028, flexible). DEADLINE: 15 September 2026. Apply via the Fulbright Scholar Program at https://fulbrightscholars.org/award/fulbright-imera-mediterranean-studies-award-aix-marseille-university.

item.title: NEH: Collections Stewardship (December 2026 cycle)
item.description:
Organisation: National Endowment for the Humanities (NEH)

Location: United States. Applicants must be US-based institutions.

Award: Up to $350,000 for single organizations; up to $500,000 for consortia. Projects up to 3 years. No application fee.

Duration: Up to 3 years.

Deadline: 15 Dec 2026

NEH Collections Stewardship funds preservation projects at libraries, archives, museums, colleges/universities, and preservation-services organisations. Eligible activities include digitisation and reformatting of collections for public access through digital repositories, preservation planning, and environmental assessments. Relevant for organisations working with born-digital or digitally reformatted collections including software, AI artefacts, and digital cultural heritage. Applications for the December 2026 cycle open 31 August 2026; deadline 15 December 2026. A May 2026 cycle has already passed. ELIGIBILITY: institutional applicants only (libraries, archives, museums, colleges, universities, preservation-services organisations based in the US); individual researchers and for-profit entities are not eligible. Apply via grants.gov.

item.title: Pioneer Works: 2027 Open Call – Visual Arts and Music Residency (Brooklyn, NY)
item.description:
Organisation: Pioneer Works

Location: Pioneer Works, Red Hook, Brooklyn, New York, USA

Award: Visual Arts: USD 5,000 one-time award (USD 2,500 honorarium + USD 500/month stipend for 5 months). Music: USD 3,000 one-time award (USD 2,500 honorarium + USD 500/month stipend for 1 month). Free studio space and facilities access. No housing or travel provided. No application fee.

Duration: Visual Arts: 5 months. Music: 1 month. Cohort begins after jury decisions in late September 2026.

Deadline: 13 Jul 2026

Pioneer Works in Red Hook, Brooklyn invites applications from emerging visual artists and musicians for its 2027 residency cohort. 12 Visual Arts residents (5-month residency) and 12 Music residents (1-month residency) are selected each year through a two-phase jury process. WHAT IS PROVIDED: free studio space; access to facilities including risograph printer, darkroom, ceramics studio, large-format printer, 3D printers, thermobinder, and laser cutter; biweekly studio visits with curators, gallerists, critics, and artists; free access to all public programmes at Pioneer Works, including Second Sundays (monthly open studios and performances). FINANCIAL AWARD: USD 5,000 for Visual Arts residents; USD 3,000 for Music residents (each comprising a USD 2,500 honorarium plus USD 500/month for the residency duration); applicants' choice on how to spend it. Pioneer Works does not provide housing, travel, or visa sponsorship. ELIGIBILITY: open locally, nationally, and internationally; collectives may apply (resources shared); current students and programme alumni are not eligible. REVIEW PROCESS: phase one is internal scoring on technical ability, uniqueness of vision, and future potential (nontraditional career paths given special consideration); highest scorers advance to an external jury of art professionals and alumni who assess diversity of demographics and media, genuine studio need, and fit with Pioneer Works' public-facing culture. Jury convenes September 2026; all applicants notified by late September. Supported by Dalio Philanthropies, the Andy Warhol Foundation, NYC Department of Cultural Affairs, New York State Council on the Arts, and the National Endowment for the Arts. APPLY: via Pioneer Works website by 13 July 2026. Questions: residency@pioneerworks.org.

Open call details

item.title: GovAI DC Winter Fellowship 2027
item.description:
Organisation: Centre for the Governance of AI (GovAI)

Location: Washington, DC, USA; in-person, full-time (part-time possible for DC-based think tank or government staff).

Award: $21,000 stipend for the three-month term, plus travel support to DC and weekday lunch provided. No visa sponsorship. No application fee.

Duration: 3 months, 18 January to 9 April 2027.

Deadline: 12 Jul 2026

GovAI's DC Winter Fellowship is a three-month bipartisan in-person fellowship in Washington, DC, designed for individuals seeking to enter or advance in US AI governance and policy. The program emphasises bipartisan engagement, rigorous analysis, and practical policy relevance, with a focus on how AI policy is shaped within the US political system. Fellows conduct independent research on a topic of their choice with mentorship from a GovAI supervisor; the first two weeks explore the AI policy landscape before a research proposal is finalised. Outputs may include a report, white paper, journal article, op-ed, or blog post targeted at an audience relevant to AI policy. Fellows also attend expert Q&As, skills workshops, seminars, work-in-progress meetings, and social events, and are encouraged to discuss follow-on career opportunities. GovAI alumni have gone on to roles in US government bodies (NSC, OSTP, Senate, Department of Commerce, DHS); think tanks (CSIS, CSET, CNAS, RAND, Heritage Foundation, FAI); AI companies (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google DeepMind); and universities (Yale, Stanford, MIT). AMOUNT: $21,000 stipend for three months, plus travel support to DC and weekday lunch. Part-time fellowship at a pro-rated salary available for DC-based think tank researchers, government staffers, or professionals doing AI governance work; pay accommodations possible. ELIGIBILITY: candidates must have the right to work in the US; no visa sponsorship. Open to a wide range of backgrounds (government, academia, industry, civil society); promising undergraduates welcome; no strict degree requirements. Relevant areas include public policy, political science, engineering, computer science, economics, biosecurity, cybersecurity, China studies, and risk management. SELECTION CRITERIA: quality and clarity of written work, relevant expertise, judgement, and team fit. APPLICATION PROCESS: (1) written application and automated abstract reasoning assessment; (2) paid remote work test (August 2026); (3) remote interview and reference checks (September 2026); decisions communicated October 2026. Application deadline not yet publicly stated; applications open June 2026 -- check programme page for the deadline.

Open call details

item.title: Claude Corps Fellowship
item.description:
Organisation: Anthropic (funded); CodePath (employer of record); Social Finance (evaluation)

Location: USA; in-person at host nonprofit organizations across America (placement location varies; relocation support available).

Award: $85,000 full-time annual salary plus benefits, expansive Claude token budget, and relocation support as needed. No application fee.

Duration: 12 months.

Deadline: 17 Jul 2026

Claude Corps is a national fellowship program from Anthropic placing early-career fellows full-time at nonprofits across America for one year, with the goal of extending AI's benefits to communities and building fellows' AI skills. Anthropic funds the program and provides Claude expertise; CodePath (America's largest collegiate CS education provider) is the fellows' official employer of record and leads programming; Social Finance leads measurement and evaluation. AMOUNT: $85,000 full-time salary plus benefits; expansive Claude token budget; relocation support as needed. FELLOW EXPERIENCE: intensive Claude training at the start; five hours of ongoing weekly training throughout; support from a CodePath mentor; Anthropic office hours for technical questions; and professional guidance from the host organization manager. SAMPLE HOST ORGANIZATIONS: Braven (Chicago), Code the Dream (Durham NC), Heartland Forward (Bentonville AR), Montgomery County Food Bank (Conroe TX), Reef Environmental Education Foundation (Key Largo FL), StriveTogether (Cincinnati), YMCA of Greater Charlotte, and more than 400 nonprofits total. ELIGIBILITY: anyone over 18 with under two years of full-time work experience, regardless of educational background; must be authorized to work in the US; must be comfortable working with Claude and willing to relocate if necessary. COHORTS: Cohort 1 (100 fellows, starts October 2026) -- applications close 17 July 2026; Cohort 2 (starts January 2027) and Cohort 3 (starts August 2027) -- rolling applications. Apply at https://www.anthropic.com/news/claude-corps.

Open call details

item.title: Hellmouth Magazine: Volume 3 "Metamorphosis" - Call for Submissions
item.description:
Organisation: Hellmouth Magazine

Location: Knoxville, TN, USA; Southeast US focus for reviews, but national and international exceptions considered. Remote submissions.

Award: $50 honorarium upon publication. No submission fee.

Duration: One-time publication in Volume 3.

Deadline: Closed 20 Jun 2026

Hellmouth Magazine, a 501(c)(3) nonprofit arts and culture publication based in Knoxville, TN, is seeking submissions for Volume 3 on the theme of "Metamorphosis." The magazine publishes twice annually, covering experimental essays, critical art writing, reviews, interviews, and visual art. HONORARIUM: $50 upon publication. CATEGORIES OPEN: Reviews (exhibitions, gallery events, artist shows, cultural happenings -- Southeast focus but national/international exceptions welcomed; 500-750 words); Interviews (artists living or dead; video interviews also welcome; open word count); Art Writing (academic or experimental essays on contemporary or historical art -- artists, movements, zeitgeist, concepts, theories; 1,200+ words); Visual Art (digital output or print; best submitted alongside an upcoming show or event). FICTION & POETRY: closed for this volume. THEME: Metamorphosis as transformative vehicle -- post-Anthropocentric systems, chimeras, the post-human and transhuman, cultural and political shapeshifting, transformation through art and literature in the wake of collective trauma. All pitches should be approximately 500 words. Read recent issues before submitting. Submissions due 20 June 2026. Submit at https://www.hellmouthmag.com/submit.

Open call details

item.title: BMI Foundation Babyface Award 2026
item.description:
Organisation: BMI Foundation

Location: USA; remote application.

Award: $10,000 career grant plus artistic and business mentorship. No application fee.

Duration: One-time award.

Deadline: Closed 30 Jun 2026

The BMI Foundation Babyface Award is an inaugural annual award supporting emerging songwriters working in R&B, hip-hop, soul, and music of the African diaspora. Founded with a $25,000 investment from Wells Fargo. AMOUNT: $10,000 career grant plus artistic and business mentorship. ELIGIBILITY: ages 18-29 as of 30 June 2026; US residents or those in US territories; submitted works must be unpublished and unassigned to major labels or publishers (self-released works are eligible); co-written works are eligible if all writers meet the eligibility criteria (maximum 3 writers; award split evenly). One application per person per year; cannot submit the same work to multiple BMI Foundation programs. No affiliation with BMI required. APPLICATION MATERIALS: a 1-2 minute personal statement video; two audio files with anonymized lyric sheets (lyrics may be in any language with English translation); and a description of the creative process (audio, visual, or written format). No application fee. Apply via Google Form at https://bmifoundation.org/by-application/babyfaceaward. Deadline: 30 June 2026.
